1: The opening date formula is precise: "In the second year of King Darius, on the first day of the sixth month" — August 29, 520 BCE by modern reckoning. The prophet Haggai is called only navi (prophet), not given any genealogy or hometown; his identity is entirely functional. The word comes "by the hand of (be-yad) Haggai" — the standard formula for prophetic delivery, where the prophet is YHWH's instrument. The recipients are Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and Joshua son of Jehozadak, the high priest — the civil and cultic leaders of the returned community. Zerubbabel appears in Matthew's genealogy (Matt 1:12-13), grandson of Jehoiachin; Joshua is the high priest whose restoration is also the subject of Zechariah 3.
2: "These people" (ha-am hazeh) — not "my people" but "this people" — is YHWH's pointed distancing from a community that has set covenant obligations aside. The claim that "the time has not yet come to rebuild YHWH's house" (lo-et-bo et-beit YHWH lehibanot) uses the same word for the temple that will dominate the book. The delay is not theological but practical — the returned community prioritized their own comfort. The et (time/right time) they invoke as their excuse becomes, in YHWH's refutation, the et they had already found convenient for their own houses.
3: A brief transition verse: "Then the word of YHWH came by the hand of the prophet Haggai" — the formula is repeated from 1:1, reinforcing that what follows is not the prophet's editorial but a direct divine word. The short verses at transition points in Haggai serve as structural markers, dividing the book into five distinct oracles (1:1-11, 1:12-15, 2:1-9, 2:10-19, 2:20-23). The repetition of the delivery formula gives each oracle its own framing as an independent word.
4: The rhetorical question — "Is it time (ha-et) for you yourselves to be living in your paneled houses (batim sephunim) while this house lies in ruins (charev)?" — pairs two et (time) references: their claim that the time hasn't come for the temple, and YHWH's counter that they've already found time for their own comfort. Batim sephunim means specifically cedar-paneled rooms — the luxury finishing Solomon used for the temple's inner rooms (1 Kgs 6:15). The returned community used temple-grade finishing for their own homes while leaving the temple itself in rubble (charev = desolate, as in a wasteland).
5: "So now this is what YHWH of Hosts says: Consider carefully your ways (simu levavkem al-darkeikhem)" — the literal Hebrew is "set your heart on your roads/paths." The phrase recurs at 1:7, 2:15, and 2:18, forming the book's characteristic refrain of self-examination. Simu levavkem (lit. "place your heart") calls for more than intellectual reflection — the lev (heart) in Hebrew is the seat of will and decision, not merely emotion. The command is to direct their full intentional attention to the pattern of their life. What have they actually been doing? What has it produced? The examination itself is the beginning of repentance.
6: The list of futilities catalogs the precise reversal of covenant blessing: "you have planted much but harvested little; you eat but never have enough; you drink but never have your fill; you clothe yourselves but no one is warm; and he who earns wages does so to put them into a bag with holes (tseror naquv)." The imagery of the bag with holes is vivid — every coin earned falls through before it can accumulate. This is the Deuteronomic covenant curse enacted in economic terms (Deut 28:38-40): labor produces nothing because the blessing that makes labor fruitful has been withdrawn. The community works but cannot accumulate; they harvest but cannot store.
7: The refrain returns: "This is what YHWH of Hosts says: Consider carefully your ways" (simu levavkem al-darkeikhem). The repetition after the list of futilities is rhetorical: having described the evidence, YHWH asks them to draw the conclusion themselves. The examination is not punitive but diagnostic — the community needs to recognize the pattern of cause and effect. Their inattention to YHWH's house is the cause; the economic futility is the effect. The simu levavkem formula invites them to make that connection rather than be told it.
8: The divine directive: "Go up to the mountain (alah ha-har), bring down timber (etzim), and build the house — so that I may take pleasure in it (ve-ertseh bo) and be glorified (ve-ekaved)." Three imperatives followed by divine purpose. The mountain is not specified — the hills around Jerusalem. The construction material is wood, not stone; the task is not the full temple project but getting it started. Ertseh (I will take pleasure/be pleased) uses the priestly vocabulary of divine acceptance — the same root as the ‘acceptable year’ and sacrificial acceptance. Ve-ekaved (I will be glorified) — the temple is the vehicle of YHWH's kavod (glory/weight/presence).
9: "You looked for much, but behold it came to little; and when you brought it home, I blew it away (nafachti vo)." The image of YHWH blowing away their gathered harvest with a breath is sharply personal — not an impersonal economic force but direct divine action. The question that follows explains: "Because of my house, which lies in ruins (charev), while each of you runs to his own house (beito)." The wordplay between beit YHWH (YHWH's house) and beito (his own house) is exact: they rush to their own beit while YHWH's beit is charev (desolate). The covenant economy inverted.
10: "Therefore on account of you (aleikhem), the skies (shamayim) have held back the dew (tal), and the earth has held back its produce (yevulah)." The economic reversal in 1:6 is now explicitly traced to its source: not natural variability but covenantal consequence. YHWH commands creation to hold back the rain-dew cycle that makes agricultural life possible. Aleikhem places the responsibility squarely on them. The withholding is not punishment but consequence — the community that withholds what belongs to YHWH finds that YHWH withholds what belongs to them. The covenant works in both directions.
11: "And I decreed a drought (chorev) on the land and on the hills, on the grain, the new wine, and the oil — on all that the ground yields — on man and on cattle and on all the labor of hands (yegiea kapayim)." Chorev (drought/desolation) is the exact root as the charev (ruined/desolate) used of the temple in 1:4,9 — the drought-desolation (chorev) falls on the land because the house-desolation (charev) was allowed to continue. The wordplay is precise and deliberate: the form of the curse mirrors the form of the neglect. The comprehensive list — grain, wine, oil, humans, cattle, all labor — covers every category of covenant blessing from Deuteronomy 7:13 and 28:18.
12: "Then Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel and Joshua son of Jehozadak the high priest and all the remnant of the people (kol she'erit ha-am) obeyed the voice of YHWH their God." The triple subject — governor, priest, all the remnant — indicates comprehensive covenant response. She'erit (remnant) is the technical term for the surviving community of the exile; their obedience is the fulfillment of what the prophets had promised the remnant would eventually do. "And they feared before YHWH" (va-yire'u et-pnei YHWH) — the fear is reverent awe at YHWH's word delivered through his messenger, not terror. This is the covenant yirah that is the beginning of wisdom and the seed of genuine worship.
13: "Then Haggai, the messenger of YHWH (malak YHWH), spoke the LORD's message to the people: 'I am with you (ani itkem), declares YHWH.'" Malak YHWH (messenger/angel of YHWH) is the title used for the divine messenger in Genesis 16, Judges 6, etc. Haggai is the only writing prophet explicitly called malak YHWH — his prophetic function is elevated to the status of divine messenger. The content of the message is the entire covenant assurance in three words: ani itkem — "I am with you." This is the Immanuel formula (Isa 7:14; Matt 1:23) concentrated to its minimum: the divine presence accompanies those who return to covenant faithfulness.
14: "And YHWH stirred up (va-yaar) the spirit (ruach) of Zerubbabel son of Shealtiel... and the spirit of Joshua son of Jehozadak the high priest, and the spirit of all the remnant of the people." The divine initiative is explicit: YHWH does not merely command but moves the human will. Va-yaar (stirred/roused/awakened) — the same root describes the stirring of the east wind and the awakening from sleep. YHWH awakens what lay dormant in each of the three groups. The response in 1:12 is human obedience; the enablement in 1:14 is divine stirring. Both are true simultaneously — genuine human response that is enabled by divine initiative. "And they came and worked on the house of YHWH of Hosts."
15: The closing date of the first oracle: "on the twenty-fourth day of the sixth month, in the second year of King Darius." The first oracle began on the first day of the sixth month (1:1); work began twenty-three days later. The precision of the dates is a feature of Haggai unique among the prophetic books — every oracle is precisely dated, creating a calendar of covenant renewal. The dates allow reconstruction: the community moved from YHWH's word (1st of 6th month) to actual construction (24th of 6th month) in less than a month. The gap between hearing and doing was brief, which Haggai records as significant.
